content.text: Python Virtual Environments: venv and virtualenv

Every Python project needs its own isolated space to run cleanly. Without isolation, installing a library for one project can silently break another — version conflicts accumulate, dependencies bleed across projects, and debugging becomes a nightmare. Virtual environments solve this by giving each project its own private Python installation and package set, completely separate from everything else on the system.

is a self-contained directory that holds a specific version of Python and a set of installed packages. When activated, it intercepts all Python and pip commands so they work only within that isolated space — nothing leaks in from the system Python, and nothing leaks out.
There are two main tools for creating virtual environments in Python:
— built into Python 3.3+, no installation required, the modern default
, supports Python 2 and 3, and offers additional features
Both tools produce the same conceptual outcome: an isolated Python environment. The differences lie in capability, speed, and Python version support.
Python has one global package installation location per interpreter by default. If Project A needs
, installing both on the system Python is impossible — one will overwrite the other.
This problem is especially severe in Generative AI and machine learning work, where:
A single version mismatch can cause silent numerical errors, import failures, or crashes at runtime
Different AI frameworks (e.g., TensorFlow vs PyTorch) may conflict over shared transitive dependencies like
Virtual environments ensure that each project gets exactly the dependencies it was built and tested with — no more, no less.
— packages installed in one environment have no effect on another
— environments can be exported and recreated exactly on other machines
— trying a new library version won't break other projects
— deleting the environment folder removes everything with no traces left behind
— you can have Python 3.10 for one project and 3.11 for another
— environments live in user space, not system directories
— build pipelines can spin up fresh environments per run, guaranteeing clean builds
— each environment duplicates the Python binary and all installed packages; a large ML project environment can easily exceed 5–10 GB
— forgetting to activate the environment is a common source of confusion; packages appear missing even though they are installed
— environments contain hardcoded absolute paths; moving or renaming the folder usually breaks them
uses the Python interpreter it was created with; changing the Python version requires recreating the environment
— for environments that mix Python packages with native binaries, system libraries, or CUDA drivers,
When a virtual environment is created, the tool:
) containing a copy or symlink of the Python interpreter
on Windows) where all installed packages go
on Windows) with Python and pip executables, plus an
When activated, the shell prepends the environment's
, the shell finds the environment's versions first — not the system ones.
script also sets an environment variable (
) that tools and scripts can check to know they're running inside a virtual environment.
is restored and everything returns to normal.

System Python (/usr/bin/python3)
│
├── Global site-packages (shared, potentially conflicting)
│
└── Virtual Environment (.venv/)
    ├── bin/
    │   ├── python          ← symlink or copy of interpreter
    │   ├── pip             ← environment-specific pip
    │   └── activate        ← shell script to activate/deactivate
    ├── lib/
    │   └── python3.x/
    │       └── site-packages/   ← ALL packages installed here
    └── pyvenv.cfg          ← metadata: base Python path, version
file is the key configuration file. It tells Python where it came from and whether it should see the system's global packages.
during creation lets the environment see globally installed packages — useful but generally avoided as it reduces isolation.
Ships with latest pip, setuptools, wheel
is sufficient. For testing across multiple Python versions (e.g., with

# Create a virtual environment in a folder named .venv
python3 -m venv .venv
name is a widely adopted convention. The folder can be named anything, but
is recognized by most editors (VS Code, PyCharm) and is often listed in
To create with a specific Python version (if multiple are installed):
After activation, the terminal prompt changes to show the environment name:
This records every installed package and its exact version:

python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
Create with a specific Python version
Create without pip (useful in CI where pip is separately managed)
Create with access to global packages
Checking You're in the Right Environment

# Confirm which Python is active
which python        # macOS/Linux
where python        # Windows

# Should print something like:
# /home/user/project/.venv/bin/python

# Confirm pip installs go to the right place
pip show numpy      # Shows location inside .venv
Example 1 — A simple AI project setup

mkdir my-llm-project && cd my-llm-project
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

pip install openai tiktoken python-dotenv
pip freeze > requirements.txt
Example 2 — Two projects with conflicting numpy versions

# Project A: needs numpy 1.x
cd projectA
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install "numpy<2.0"
deactivate

# Project B: needs numpy 2.x
cd projectB
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install "numpy>=2.0"
deactivate
Both coexist without conflict because each has its own isolated
Example 3 — Using virtualenv for cross-version testing

virtualenv --python=python3.9 .venv39
virtualenv --python=python3.11 .venv311

source .venv39/bin/activate
pip install pytest
pytest tests/
deactivate

source .venv311/bin/activate
pip install pytest
pytest tests/
deactivate

A team building a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline uses a monorepo with three services: an ingestion service, an embedding service, and an API service. Each has conflicting dependency requirements:
, which is compatible with either but prefers
Without virtual environments, these three cannot coexist on the same machine. With virtual environments:

monorepo/
├── ingestion/
│   ├── .venv/          ← pydantic 1.x installed here
│   └── requirements.txt
├── embedding/
│   ├── .venv/          ← pydantic 2.x installed here
│   └── requirements.txt
└── api/
    ├── .venv/          ← pydantic 2.x installed here
    └── requirements.txt
Each service runs with exactly the packages it needs. The CI/CD pipeline recreates each environment from its
on every build, ensuring reproducibility across development machines and production servers.
without activating the environment installs into the global Python or another active environment. Always confirm activation with
The environment contains absolute paths specific to one machine. It should never be committed. Add it to
shows packages in a readable format but is not suitable for
5. Including development-only tools in production requirements
are for development only. Shipping them to production inflates image sizes and increases attack surface. Use separate

pip install -r requirements.txt          # production deps
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt      # dev/test deps
without a version pin means a future
may install a different, potentially incompatible version. Always freeze after a working install.
Virtual environments contain hardcoded paths. Moving
to a different location breaks them. Recreate rather than move.
— this is the most widely recognized convention and is auto-detected by VS Code, PyCharm, and most linters
— never share an environment across multiple projects
to capture the full exact state
Create the environment at the project root
— long-lived environments can accumulate conflicting packages; rebuilding from
— even a simple note like "Requires Python 3.11" prevents hours of debugging
Virtual environments themselves have negligible runtime overhead — activation is just a
change and adds no latency to Python execution.
Disk usage is the main concern at scale:

In containerized deployments (Docker), virtual environments are often unnecessary because the container itself provides isolation. However, using a virtual environment inside a Docker container is still a valid and common pattern because it ensures
writes to a predictable, non-root location and makes layer caching more effective.

FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
RUN python -m venv .venv
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN .venv/bin/pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD [".venv/bin/python", "main.py"]
directly — this avoids the need to activate the environment in a Docker context.
In CI/CD pipelines, environments should be cached between runs (using the
hash as a cache key) to avoid reinstalling gigabytes of packages on every commit.
internally, adds lock files and build tools
Manages virtual environments as part of a broader workflow
with native binary support; common in data science
Drop-in replacement written in Rust; 10–100x faster installs
deserves special mention as of 2024–2025. It is a Rust-based tool that replaces both

# Install uv
pip install uv

# Create environment
uv venv .venv

# Install packages (dramatically faster than pip)
uv pip install torch transformers
and is increasingly adopted in production AI workflows where install times matter.
No native binary or CUDA management
For Generative AI work involving GPUs, CUDA, and cuDNN,
inside the conda environment manages Python packages.
, malicious packages with the same name as internal packages can be substituted from PyPI. Mitigations include using
to point to a private package registry and verifying package hashes with
reduces (but does not eliminate) risk. For high-security environments, combine pinning with hash verification:
— Old environments may contain packages with known CVEs. Regularly audit installed packages:
— The Python interpreter symlinked inside the environment points back to the system Python. If the system Python is updated or removed, the environment may break. Using tools like
to manage Python versions separately provides a more stable base.
— Automated scripts often fail silently when the environment is not activated. Instead of relying on activation, reference the environment's Python directly:
This is the most reliable approach for CI/CD, cron jobs, and Docker containers.
How Python Finds Packages: The Import System
Virtual environments work by manipulating Python's

— the ordered list of directories Python searches when you import a module. Understanding this mechanism explains both how virtual environments isolate packages and what goes wrong when activation is forgotten.
from several sources: the script's directory, the
directory of the current Python interpreter. The
module (auto-imported at startup) locates and adds the interpreter's

import sys

# Without a virtual environment active:
# sys.path includes the system Python's site-packages
print('\n'.join(sys.path))
# /usr/lib/python3.11
# /usr/lib/python3.11/lib-dynload
# /usr/local/lib/python3.11/dist-packages   ← global packages

# With a virtual environment active:
# sys.path starts with the environment's site-packages
# /home/user/project/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages   ← venv packages first
# /usr/lib/python3.11
# /usr/lib/python3.11/lib-dynload
# (global dist-packages is NOT included by default)
When a virtual environment is activated, two key changes happen:
environment variable is set to the environment's root path. The
module detects this at startup and adds the environment's

# Inspect sys.path before and after activation
python3 -c "import sys; print('\n'.join(sys.path))"

source .venv/bin/activate
python3 -c "import sys; print('\n'.join(sys.path))"

# Also: check VIRTUAL_ENV is set
echo $VIRTUAL_ENV
# /home/user/project/.venv

# Tools like pytest, mypy, black detect VIRTUAL_ENV and use it
# to find the correct Python environment automatically
fails after installing a package: if you installed the package into the system Python but the virtual environment is active (or vice versa), the directory containing the package is not on
dependencies — version ranges that define compatibility constraints, not exact locks. This is the modern standard for distributable packages and is increasingly used for applications too.

[project]
name = "my-llm-app"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.10"
dependencies = [
    "torch>=2.0",
    "transformers>=4.30,<5",
    "numpy>=1.24",
    "fastapi>=0.100",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=7",
    "black",
    "mypy",
    "ipykernel",
]

[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"
defining abstract requirements, the workflow with

# Create and activate environment
python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate

# Install the project itself in editable mode (includes all dependencies)
pip install -e .

# Install dev extras
pip install -e '.[dev]'

# Generate a locked requirements.txt for reproducible deploys
pip freeze > requirements-lock.txt

# Alternatively, use pip-compile from pip-tools for a cleaner workflow
# pip install pip-tools
# pip-compile pyproject.toml -o requirements-lock.txt
(exact pins in the lock file), enabling both flexibility for downstream users and reproducibility for deployment.

A common question is whether to use a virtual environment inside Docker. The container already provides OS-level isolation, so a virtual environment inside Docker might seem redundant. However, using one has concrete advantages:
Installing to a venv inside Docker avoids
warnings introduced in Python 3.12+, where pip refuses to install globally without explicit permission.
Copying requirements.txt before the rest of the application source and running
into a venv makes Docker layer caching more effective: the dependency install layer is only invalidated when
changes, not when application code changes.
Running the container as a non-root user (recommended for security) can write to a venv inside the working directory without needing to modify system paths.

FROM python:3.11-slim

# Create a non-root user for security
RUN useradd --create-home appuser
USER appuser
WORKDIR /home/appuser/app

# Create venv in a location the non-root user owns
RUN python -m venv .venv

# Install dependencies in the venv BEFORE copying application code
# This ensures Docker caches the dep install layer separately
COPY --chown=appuser requirements.txt .
RUN .venv/bin/pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code (invalidates only the last layer on code change)
COPY --chown=appuser . .

# Use venv Python directly — no activation needed in Dockerfile
CMD [".venv/bin/python", "-m", "uvicorn", "app:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0"]

Machine learning projects have specific environment management challenges due to the size of dependencies (PyTorch alone can exceed 2 GB) and the need for GPU drivers that pip cannot manage. Here are proven patterns:
Pattern 1: Pure pip with GPU PyTorch

python3.11 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# Install PyTorch with CUDA 12.1 support
# The --index-url flag points pip to PyTorch's own package index
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio \
    --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

# Install remaining ML packages from PyPI
pip install transformers accelerate datasets peft
pip freeze > requirements.txt
Pattern 2: conda for CUDA + pip for Python packages

# Use conda to install CUDA toolkit and base Python
conda create -n myproject python=3.11 cuda-toolkit=12.1 -c nvidia
conda activate myproject

# Use pip INSIDE the conda env for Python packages
# This avoids conda's slow resolver for PyPI packages
pip install torch transformers accelerate

# Export the full environment
conda env export > environment.yml

# Recreate on another machine
conda env create -f environment.yml
Pattern 3: uv for fast dependency resolution

# uv is a Rust-based pip/venv replacement — 10-100x faster
pip install uv

# Create environment (same structure as venv)
uv venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# Install packages (dramatically faster — uv resolves and downloads in parallel)
uv pip install torch transformers accelerate datasets

# Compile a lock file from pyproject.toml
uv pip compile pyproject.toml -o requirements.lock

# Install from lock file
uv pip sync requirements.lock
# 'sync' removes packages not in the lock file — ensures exact environment

command is particularly valuable in CI/CD — it not only installs all required packages but also removes any packages present in the environment that are not in the lock file, ensuring a clean and reproducible environment even when re-using a cached environment directory.
Virtual environments give each Python project its own isolated package space, eliminating version conflicts
is built into Python 3 and is the standard choice for new projects;
adds Python 2 support and extra features
Activation works by prepending the environment's
Always add the environment folder to
to capture the exact state of an environment for reproducibility
In Docker and CI environments, invoke the environment's Python binary directly rather than relying on shell activation
